claim: Scaling sparse autoencoders from small language models to Claude 3 Sonnet required distribution across multiple GPUs using op-sharing (parameter distribution) and massive data shuffling—the math is simple but the engineering is hard
actionable: When scaling interpretability or training infrastructure: identify bottlenecks through profiling, implement multi-stage parallel shuffling for petabyte-scale data, use op-sharing for large parameter sets, and focus on the 20% of engineering that gives 80% speedup

claim: Data shuffling for machine learning at petabyte scale requires multi-stage parallel shuffle: break problem recursively into smaller stages, shuffle each stage in parallel, then mix sections in provable ways to ensure all sections mix with all others
actionable: For large-scale ML data preprocessing, implement hierarchical parallel shuffling: divide data into warehouses → shuffle within warehouses → shuffle between warehouses using a recursive merge-shuffle pattern; frame the problem correctly (90% of the work)

claim: Research engineering differs from product engineering: requirements constantly change, you don't know which code you'll delete vs keep, and the tradeoff between short-term speed and long-term code quality is constantly shifting under you
actionable: In research ML projects, maintain separate effort budgets: invest heavily in code quality for core components you'll use for months+, but keep experimental code hacky; regularly re-evaluate which pieces warrant cleanup based on what's working

claim: Correctness bugs in ML code are extremely dangerous because evaluation metrics can be subtly broken in ways that make false results look too good to believe—spending weeks chasing impressive numbers that later turn out to be metric bugs
actionable: For ML research: log every possible metric during training, graph them all, compare against what you theoretically expect, manually review code against the math, and when a result seems impossibly good, suspect a metric bug before trusting it

claim: Sparse autoencoders work better than more complex mathematical techniques for feature extraction at scale because they are simple and truly scalable—the bitter lesson applies: simple algorithms with massive scale beat complicated algorithms
actionable: When choosing ML techniques for scaling interpretability or other areas, pick simple algorithms that have been proven to scale cleanly rather than mathematically sophisticated ones that haven't been battle-tested at your data scale

claim: Discovered multimodal, multilingual features in Claude 3 Sonnet—single neurons fire for the same concept across text and images (e.g., 'veganism' concept appears in images of plant food, text about veganism, and concepts across languages)
actionable: Understand that large language models have learned cross-modal concept representations; when building safety evaluations or interpretability tools, account for the fact that harmful concepts may be represented consistently across modalities

claim: A single feature in Claude detected both code backdoors and images of hidden cameras in everyday objects—suggesting models have abstract understanding of 'hidden malicious functionality' rather than just pattern matching
actionable: When thinking about model alignment and safety, recognize that LLMs likely understand abstract concepts (deception, harm, violation) rather than just repeating training data; this matters for how you evaluate adversarial scenarios

claim: When a new ML idea underperforms during implementation, it's hard to know if the idea was bad or if the implementation was buggy—requires staring at code and graphs to debug before abandoning the idea
actionable: For each ML experiment that underperforms: (1) carefully audit implementation against the math, (2) graph all intermediate computations, (3) if you still can't find the bug after a day, either abandon the idea or get a code review from someone expert in that area

claim: Research team effectiveness comes from complementary skillsets: mathematicians, engineers, and scientists collaborating—each can spot optimizations (math: statistics, engineering: parallelization, science: problem framing) that the others miss
actionable: When hiring or forming research teams, explicitly value complementary skills; create time for pairing sessions where people with different specialties work together; often the math solution, the engineering solution, and the science solution are all needed

claim: For interpretability research to succeed, you need breadth of engineering skills—not depth in one area; the bottleneck constantly moves, so being able to quickly identify the current bottleneck and fix it in days (not months) matters more than perfect optimization in any one area
actionable: When hiring ML/interpretability engineers, prioritize breadth and debugging speed over depth; value people who can profile, identify the bottleneck, and fix it quickly rather than specialists who spend months optimizing one thing

claim: Full-stack research infrastructure—spanning GPU kernels to frontend Figma design—is critical; a bug anywhere in the stack (even frontend) can mask GPU optimization issues, requiring investigation across the entire pipeline
actionable: Build and maintain integrated monitoring across your entire research pipeline; when performance degrades, don't assume it's the algorithm—profile the full stack (data loading, compute, visualization, database) before optimizing any single layer

claim: Pull engineering work forward in the research timeline to increase iteration speed—making experiments run in an hour instead of a week matters more than having slightly better ideas if no one can validate them quickly
actionable: Before optimizing your algorithms, optimize your experimental infrastructure to reduce feedback loops from days→hours→minutes; this often gives 10-100x speedup in research velocity
